figures.caption: Figure 6 | All F1 (BoN): Thinking consistently outperforms the baselines often matching the ICL performance.
Figure 7 | Scaling with N: Thinking scales better with more samples. In the out-of-distribution case, Augmentation fails to generalize completely similar to Finetuning.
Figure 1 | Comparison between train-time augmentation strategies vs test-time thinking to improve latent generalization.
Figure 5 | Effect of RL (without CoT reasoning) on baselines. All-F1 (top row), Train Recall (bottom row)

Figure 2 | Semantic Structure Experiment: F1 scores on in- and out-of-distribution holdouts. Both Finetuning and Augmentation undergo RL training as described in Section 3.2. Thinking shows significant and consistent improvement over the baselines on both in- and out-of-distribution holdout splits, particularly for those splits that require multi-hop reasoning. Train-time augmentations completely fail to generalize on a new knowledge structures (out-of-distribution generalization).
Figure 9 | Latent Generalization Metrics (BoN): In-Distribution (left), Out-Distribution (Right).
Figure 4 | Semantic Structure Benchmark: Train set examples of Wikipedia style documents and qa examples (left) and test set example (right).

Figure 3 | Pass@N Accuracy: Exact string match accuracy of reversal curse paper dataset. Finetuning completely fails to generalize even with multiple attempts. Thinking gives non-trivial generalization but overall for such strict reversals ICL is the strongest baseline.
Figure 8 | Latent Generalization Metrics: In-Distribution (left), Out-Distribution (Right). When model is sampled only once.
